text: The laparoscope is introduced through the Palmer's point into the abdomen, revealing minimal adhesion of the omentum to the anterior abdominal wall. The surgeon performs adhesiolysis using a harmonic device, addressing a small skin fistulization by removing previous proline sutures causing a recurrent stitch abscess.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis using a harmonic device to separate the omentum from the anterior abdominal wall. An assistant applies pressure from above to reduce the hernia bulge, facilitating access to the hernia sac contents and ensuring no remnants of omentum remain inside. The surgical team checks for any omental bubble contained between the loops of the omentum.

The surgeon carefully separates the periphery of the adhesion, releasing the omentum from the hernia sac. Using a harmonic device, the remaining omental tissue is pulled out, assisted by gravity due to the two-port laparoscopic approach. Fat content from the peritoneum is visible, distinct from omental fat. A proline knot causing a stitch abscess is identified and excised.

The surgeon removes a knot associated with a stitch abscess and prepares a 15x15 cm mesh for trans-facial fixation. The mesh is mapped on the skin, and points are marked approximately two centimeters lateral to its corners. A small two-millimeter prick is made for inserting a suture passer or needle to secure the pre-tied vicryl corners of the mesh.

The surgeon fixes the lateral two corners of the mesh with trans-facial sutures, ensuring careful placement to avoid pricking the inferior epigastric vessels. The third corner of the mesh is stretched and positioned two centimeters laterally to prevent corrugation and ensure it remains taut even after the removal of pneumoperitoneum gas.

The surgeon uses a simple, uncoated polypropylene mesh for the hernia repair. The fourth corner of the mesh is being secured, with the suture passing through the skin and subcutaneously holding the rectus muscle.

The surgeon tightens all corners of the polypropylene mesh, ensuring it is completely stretched and symmetrically placed. The ProTac auto suture tacker is used to fix the mesh at the corners, maintaining a two-centimeter distance between each tack for both outer and inner crowning.

The surgeon discusses the choice of mesh materials, mentioning the use of polyurethane mesh for epigastric hernias. The falciform ligament is visible but not cut, and the mesh is positioned to extend six centimeters beyond the defect's healthy margin to prevent recurrence. The inner crowning of the mesh is complete, and the surgery concludes with the gradual removal of gas. The palmar point is marked with a ten mm incision, and a five mm incision is made in the lumbar area on the left side.
